coredata.dc:title: Hybrid data-based modeling for the prediction and diagnostics of Li-ion battery thermal behaviors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Legala, Adithya
Li, Xianguo
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion battery (LIB) has been deployed for the electrification of the transport sector as a key strategy for climate change mitigation and adaptation. However, it has significant technical challenges such as thermal runaway, requiring a good understanding and accurate prediction of the LIB thermal behavior (heat generation rate). In this study, a novel hybrid approach using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is developed for predicting the heat generation rate with discharge current, output voltage, ambient temperature, cell surface temperature, and Depth of Discharge (DOD) as the feature vectors (inputs); where the DOD is estimated with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), and direct Coulomb Counting (CC) method, respectively. A shallow neural network utilizing the Marquette-Levenberg algorithm is designed and calibrated using over 8000 cases of the testing data. It is shown that the predicted heat generation rate of LIB agrees well with the experimental results with an accuracy of R > 0.995. Further potential of this hybrid data-based model is evaluated by simulating a thermal management system control and by introducing voltage and current sensor faults for diagnostic purposes. It is shown that, when compared to the experimental value, the relative error of the total heat output generated is less than 2% when there is no sensor fault, and greater than 50% and 25%, respectively, with an induced failure of the current and voltage sensor, demonstrating the ability to build accurate models relying solely on LIB discharge data for sensor diagnostics. This study highlights the combination of using battery thermal behavior with machine learning for real time battery system monitoring, controls and field diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Artificial neural network
Heat generation
Kalman filter
Energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EKF prediction case 1: measurement noise > process noise
EKF prediction case 2: measurement noise < process noise
EKF prediction case 3: faulty initialization of the state

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOC estimation based on the artificial neural network (ANN) and extended Kalman filter (EKF) (a) [34], (b) [35].
Schematic of the lithium ion battery (LIB) experimental setup with the calorimeter.
Schematic of the proposed artificial neural network (ANN) architecture with extended Kalman filter (EKF).

Battery voltage during discharge expressed as a function of the depth of discharge (DOD) as the measurement function, based on the correlation of experimental results [34] in terms of an 8th order polynomial.

(a) Regression plots of all the hyperparameter iterations tabulated in Table 2 Fig. 5(b) The artificial neural network (ANN) design for the lithium-ion battery (LIB) thermal model Fig. 5(c) Graphical representation of Tansig function(red) and its derivative(blue).

Comparison of the DOD estimation by the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and direct Coulomb counting (CC) method with the experimentally measured data by the automated battery test station (ABTS) for the case of the measurement noise larger than the process noise; magnified view (500–2500 s) on the right.

Comparison of the DOD estimation by the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and direct Coulomb counting (CC) method with the experimentally measured data by the automated battery test station (ABTS) for the case of the measurement noise less than the process noise; magnified view (850–950 s) on the right.

Comparison of the DOD estimation by the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and direct Coulomb counting (CC) method with the experimentally measured data by the automated battery test station (ABTS) when the initial values of DOD are estimated significantly incorrectly; magnified view (0–1500 s) on the right.

Comparison of the DOD estimation by the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and direct Coulomb counting (CC) method with the experimentally measured data by the automated battery test station (ABTS) when the initial values of DOD are estimated significantly incorrectly, but the parameters in the EKF are properly tuned.
A standalone ANN model performance for LIB thermal behavior (heat generation rate): (a)ANN accuracy for individual data points; and (b) ANN prediction vs true value.

CC-ANN and EKF-ANN model performance for LIB heat generation rate: (a) instantaneous heat generation rate, (b) total heat output generated, (c) relative error of the total heat output generated between the ANN models and the experimental results.
Thermal management system performance based on LIB thermal EKF-ANN model.

The performance of the ANN model predictions with current sensor fault through comparison with the respective experimental results; (a) DOD; (b) Instantaneous heat generation rate; (c) Total heat output generated, (d) Relative error of total heat output generated.

Deviation of the ANN model with voltage sensor fault; (a) EKF DOD prediction, (b) Instantaneous heat generation rate for the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN output, (c) Total heat output generated, (d) Relative error of total heat output generated.
Design parameters of the artificial neural network (ANN) for lithium-ion battery (LIB) thermal model.
The extended Kalman filter (EKF) iterations and tuning parameters.

row.entry: R > Q (faulty state initialization – unknown initial state)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative error for the final heat output prediction value of all the models.

head.ce:title: Hybrid data-based modeling for the prediction and diagnostics of Li-ion battery thermal behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, N2L 3G1, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, N2L 3G1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery (LIB) has been deployed for the electrification of the transport sector as a key strategy for climate change mitigation and adaptation. However, it has significant technical challenges such as thermal runaway, requiring a good understanding and accurate prediction of the LIB thermal behavior (heat generation rate). In this study, a novel hybrid approach using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is developed for predicting the heat generation rate with discharge current, output voltage, ambient temperature, cell surface temperature, and Depth of Discharge (DOD) as the feature vectors (inputs); where the DOD is estimated with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), and direct Coulomb Counting (CC) method, respectively. A shallow neural network utilizing the Marquette-Levenberg algorithm is designed and calibrated using over 8000 cases of the testing data. It is shown that the predicted heat generation rate of LIB agrees well with the experimental results with an accuracy of R > 0.995. Further potential of this hybrid data-based model is evaluated by simulating a thermal management system control and by introducing voltage and current sensor faults for diagnostic purposes. It is shown that, when compared to the experimental value, the relative error of the total heat output generated is less than 2% when there is no sensor fault, and greater than 50% and 25%, respectively, with an induced failure of the current and voltage sensor, demonstrating the ability to build accurate models relying solely on LIB discharge data for sensor diagnostics. This study highlights the combination of using battery thermal behavior with machine learning for real time battery system monitoring, controls and field diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Current uncertainty in oil prices, rising greenhouse gasses, and vehicle emission regulations are significantly changing the market outlook of the automobile industry [1–4]. The proposed changes intend on replacing internal combustion engine vehicles with electrified vehicles, predominantly with battery electric vehicles (BEV) [4–7]. The battery is the most prevalent electrochemical energy storage device that converts chemical energy to electrical energy and vice versa during discharge and charge operations, involving the insertion (lithiation) and extraction (delithiation) of lithium ions [8–10]. Lithium-ion-based chemistries are the most commercialized battery technologies in the automotive and portable electronics industries primarily due to their high-power density, energy density, stability, and minimal memory effect [11]. However, Lithium-ion battery (LIB) operation generates heat due to various overpotentials and electrochemical reactions associated with charging and discharging [10]. This heat generation of the LIB follows an exponential function and raises the temperature of the battery which in turn increases exothermic chemical reaction resulting in a further increase in temperature leading to the phenomena referred to as thermal runaway [12–15]. The knowledge of battery heat generation rate is essential to designing an efficient thermal management system to improve the battery performance and associated fail-safe safety systems [16,17]. LIB heat generation rate during discharge depends primarily on discharge current, voltage, ambient temperature, cell surface temperature, and the State of Charge (SOC)/Depth of Discharge (DOD) of LIB (which represents the currently available battery capacity) [10,18]. Therefore, prediction, diagnostics and prognostics of LIB thermal behavior is of significance for safe and efficient operation in practice.

Traditional battery thermal behavior models are dominated by physics-based models such as Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), lumped thermal models and heat transfer models which rely on material conduction properties, cooling system attributes and heat transfer mechanics to estimate the total heat output of the battery [19–22]. These physics-based modeling techniques though accurate cannot be used in a real-time system for field monitoring or control due to the complex computation involved, thereby limiting their use only to the developmental phase. Data-based models (AI-based) are in contrast with the physics-based models where the entire input-output relations of the model are determined experimentally without prior knowledge of material conduction properties, and internal attributes and can also be implemented in real time on the field due to their fast computation [23–27]. Considering the advantages this study focuses on the hybrid approach of using data-based models supplemented with physics-based/empirical-equivalent model techniques to predict the heat generation aspect of the LIB.

The LIB system is an electrochemical system that consists of complex chemical kinetics and ion transport phenomena, however, since it is a closed system there are no external reactant input or product exhaust parameters, relatively reducing the number of available feature vectors for the development of the data-based models. There are only four major measurement parameters in a LIB system: operating current, temperature, voltage, and total resistance/impedance, which are used typically in numerous machine learning models in this area to predict the State of Charge (SOC) or State of Health (SOH) [28,29].

Wang [30] has used a Convolution Neural Network (CNN) and linear regression to predict the internal temperature of the LIB pack, here the external temperature measurement was used as input feature vectors to predict the internal temperatures within the LIB pack on a real-time basis with a mean square error of 0.047. Shashank [18] has implemented a neural network-based model to predict the heat generated by a LIB under various ambient temperatures and nominal capacities, and the DOD, nominal capacity, ambient temperature, and discharge rate are used as the feature vectors for a feed-forward neural network with one hidden layer consisting of six neurons and the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm in the MATLAB to predict the heat generated in LiFePO4 pouch cell with good accuracy. Qian [31] has optimized the heat dissipation aspect of the LIB system using a combination of neural networks and the computational fluid dynamics (CFD) method where CFD is used to simulate the flow field and temperature field of the LIB at different battery spacing. These spacing parameters and resultant temperatures are used to train the neural network to predict the optimal battery spacing and temperature difference demonstrating a correlation between battery spacing and temperature gradient across different cells and predicted temperature difference at various spacing combinations.

There are a few cases where both ANN and various versions of Kalman Filter (KF) have been used in conjunction to model LIB specifically to improve the estimation of SOC. Multiple versions of KF are already adopted in the automotive industry and consumer electronics for LIB energy management. Wei and Nicholas [32] have used an ANN model to predict the SOC of LiFePO4 by using the current, voltage, and temperature of the battery which is coupled with an unscented Kalman filter (UKF) to reduce the associated errors of ANN during operation. Amin [33] has demonstrated the use of KF, EKF, and augmented Kalman filter along with ANN to accurately predict the SOC and state of health (SOH) of the LIB where the Kalman estimates the internal parameters of the equivalent circuit models. Similarly, both Chen [34] and Dao [35] have integrated the EKF (which estimates the SOC of the previous time step) with an ANN to predict the current SOC of the LIB, schematics for both systems can be seen in Fig. 1 respectively.

Currently, most of the existing physics-based and data-based models in the literature, either focus on the accuracy of the SOC/DOD prediction or the thermal behavior of the LIB system. However, it is evident from the literature that both DOD and thermal behavior are interdependent. Therefore, it is important to develop a LIB model that simultaneously estimates the DOD and heat generation rate in real-time, and the hybrid data-based models are uniquely poised for this purpose. The objective of this study is therefore to develop a hybrid data-based model (a combination of EKF-ANN and CC-ANN) that can accurately predict the thermal behavior of the LIB, which can also be used for real-time controls, and diagnostics. Here the ANN predicts the instantaneous heat generation rate by utilizing the DOD estimate from the EKF, and CC, respectively. This type of modeling with ANN and EKF can be regarded as a hybrid model as it incorporates both the characteristics of data-based modeling and elements of equivalent circuit, physics-based modeling. From the above-mentioned literature, it is evident that the SOC estimation through ANN and EKF is an established technique that can be further modified to predict the thermal behavior of the LIB.

The data for the development and calibration of the ANN and EKF model in MATLAB is obtained experimentally by discharging a commercially available prismatic Li-ion battery (LiFePO4) from A123 at various operating conditions [36,37]. The heat generation rate of the 20 Ah LiFePO4 prismatic battery from A123 during discharge is measured with the help of a custom-built calorimeter using High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) and a schematic of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 2
[36]. The charge and discharge cycles of the battery are controlled by Greenlight Innovations G12–200 multichannel battery test station also referred to as the Automated Battery Test Station (ABTS) with an accuracy of around 0.2 A and 0.05 V in source current and voltage control, respectively; and 0.03 A in current sink control, and 0.06 A in current measurement. The heat generation rate is calculated with the help of the convolution theorem [36]. The battery discharge tests were conducted at various discharge rates (0.25C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C) and various ambient operating temperatures (−10 °C, 0 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C). Further description and explanation of the experimental setup are published in [36]. The following test conditions are chosen to replicate the expected battery's application environment and operating conditions.

The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) modeling of LIB thermal behavior requires either State of Charge (SOC) / Depth of Discharge (DOD) along with other parameters as the inputs to the ANN models. While other parameters such as battery voltage, current drawn from the battery, ambient temperature and battery surface temperature can be measured for the training and validation of the ANN models developed, in practice SOC/DOD cannot be measured directly, though can be estimated based on other directly measurable quantities. SOC/DOD can be estimated by the Coulomb Counting (CC) method however this CC method requires accurate knowledge of the initial SOC/DOD, solely relies on the current sensor and is highly subjected to sensor noises (error accumulates over time). Another approach to estimating SOC/DOD is by using the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) technique which relies upon a voltage sensor and does not require the knowledge of the initial SOC/DOD. This study presents a novel approach of using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for predicting the heat generation rate coupled with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to estimate the corresponding SOC/DOD of the LIB, parallelly an ANN coupled with a direct CC model is also developed in conjunction to aid in the controls and diagnostics process as shown in Fig. 3 .

Two different EKF estimation models are designed based on the state equations derived from (i) the equivalent circuit model and (ii) the Coulomb counting equation, respectively, to predict the SOC/DOD. The inputs to the system are discharge current, battery output voltage, ambient temperature, and cell surface temperatures. The predicted outputs are DOD, heat generated, and control system (cooling) output, which is represented by the status of the radiator fan. A current sensor fault and voltage sensor fault are virtually simulated by altering the respective data signal with an offset (bias) to evaluate the diagnostics aspect of the data-based model. SOC can be correlated to the depth of discharge (DOD) as it is an observation from an opposite perspective change in the current direction (charge vs discharge), the sum of SOC and DOD is always equal to 1 so that the DOD can be predicted by the SOC algorithms [38]. To simplify from here DOD is the only entity stated in the formulation (modifying the existing SOC algorithms) to maintain commonality with the literature, physically we are estimating the DOD now. The relation between the SOC and DOD is illustrated as follows, the summation of SOC and DOD is always 1, i.e.,
SOC + DOD
= 1 ;
a DOD of 0.2 is equal to a SOC of 0.8. The representation of output current from the battery is represented as negative when referring to SOC and positive when referring to DOD. Under a constant discharge, the SOC of the battery tends to zero and the DOD of the battery approaches 1, for a fully charged battery the initial SOC is 1 whereas the DOD is zero, SOC-DOD correlations are tabulated in Table 1 .

This hybrid development for the prediction of heat generation can be mainly divided into two parts, the first one is the development and tuning of the EKF to predict the DOD, and the second one is to develop and calibrate the artificial neural network by using the output of EKF and other parameters to predict the heat generation rate. That is, (i) State estimation using EKF for the DOD, and (ii) Predicting the output using ANN with the output being the LIB heat generation.

Kalman Filter (KF) is one of the most prevalent and mature techniques to estimate the dynamic state of the linear system by correlating the system's estimated output parameter with the sensor measurement value. The estimated state is more accurate than the sensor measurement value, which contains measurement uncertainties (or noise). The KF algorithm considers the input data and output data to estimate the system state (x̂k) such that it minimizes the mean squared error between the estimated system state and the true system state (xk). KF assumes that the process (state equation) noise and measurement (sensor) noise are independent and acts as the optimum state estimator for the system. In the case of a non-linear system, linearization of the system at every time step (discretized) is necessary for better approximation, this aspect of linearization followed by KF implementation is commonly referred to as Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). The discretization time is calibrated according to the requirements of the system. The LIB system is considered as a non-linear system and a non-linear system is modeled as follows
(1)
x
k + 1
= f
(
x k
,
u k
)
+
w k
(2)
y k
= g
(
x k
,
u k
)
+
v k
where
x k
,
u k
,
and
y k
represent the state of the system, input to the system and system output, respectively, at an instance k . Eq. (1) is referred to as the state equation and Eq. (2) is regarded as the measurement equation.
w k
and
v k
are the independent process noise and measurement noise whose covariance matrices are represented by Q and R. For the current LIB system, there is only one state that needs to be estimated which is the DOD. Typically, DOD can be estimated by two methods: the first one is the Coulomb counting (CC) method which is a direct calculation of total charge relying solely on the current sensor reading:
(3)
D O
D T
= I n i t i a l _ D O D +
∫
t 0
T
I
( τ )
C
d τ
where t 0 is the initial time, T is the total time, I(τ) is the current and C is the total capacity of the battery during operation. The second one is an estimation algorithm, in this case, the EKF algorithm serves this purpose where the state function which is in terms of DOD is expressed by either considering a physics-based Coulomb counting (CC) derived state equation or an equivalent circuit model (ECM) based state equation coupled with a measurement equation derived from experimental data [39]. In this study, both approaches (state function) are modeled and explored.

The CC method can be modified accordingly to represent a state equation by monitoring the amount of current drawn from the battery, thereby calculating the total charge output and adding it to the previous DOD value [40] as follows:
(4)
DO
D
k + 1
= DO
D k
+
(
Δ
t
C n
)
i k
+
w k
where
DO
D k
is an inner state of the battery system,
i k
is the measured input current,
Δ
t
is the time interval and
C n
presents the cell nominal capacity. The process noise associated with the equation and its associated covariance initially need to be estimated and tuned. The nominal capacity is a function of the operating temperature and age of the LIB system which is determined by the experimental results.

The equivalent circuit of the LIB is modeled as a voltage in series with a resistance and capacitor, relatively reducing the number of the internal battery parameters thereby reducing the associated computational complexity when compared to the first order or second order RC circuits [41–43]. Capacitance and resistance values of the battery are obtained by experimentally evaluating the battery at specific temperatures and discharge rates. Where Voc is the open-circuit voltage, Rbatt is the internal resistance and Qbatt is the total capacity of the battery all of which are determined by experimental data, and output battery power (Pbatt) can be expressed as the difference between the reversible electrical power and power due to heat loss, mathematically expressed as follows [43]:
(5)
P batt
=
V OC
I batt
−
I
batt
2
R batt

The battery DOD using the Coulomb counting can be determined as follows
(6)
d DOD
d t

b a t t
Q batt

Considering the Eqs. (5) and (6) the following DOD equation can be derived [43]:
(7)

R batt

Q batt
+
w k
where Pbatt is also expressed as a function of DOD i.e.,
P batt
= (
m * DOD + C )
, where m and C are determined from the experimental results ( m = −5.096 and C = 66.488) during the implementation of the EKF algorithm. The process noise associated with the equation and associated covariance initially needs to be estimated and tuned appropriately.

The measurement function which is the voltage output of the battery is derived from the experimental data (discharge curve of LIB for a specific discharge rate and temperature) and an accurate equation is framed using MATLAB curve fitting toolbox (8th order polynomial) where the final voltage is expressed in terms of DOD as shown in Fig. 4 . This measurement equation can also be modeled by using the equivalent circuit equation, however, for the steady state operation considered in this study a direct measurement function is framed by using the experimental data, as follows:
(8)

where
x k
represents the DOD at the time instant k, and the values of the measurement function coefficients are given below:

Here are the steps involved in the implementation of the EKF algorithm for the physics-based state equation in the determination of DOD [34]:

are the predicted variables in this case state, and error covariance, respectively, at the corresponding instances whereas the

, and

are the final variables states (DOD) and error covariance, respectively, at the corresponding time instants.

ANN is one of the widely used machine learning technique based on the biological neuron, where a combination of neurons embedded with various activation functions process the computational activity to correlate the input and output attributes of the network. ANN model is designed for its architecture with respect to the number and type of hidden layers and nodes (neurons), the specific activation functions, training goals and numerical algorithms; and it is implemented by using the MATLAB NN toolbox, considering the ease of integration within SIMULINK environment where over 8000 data points are being fed from LIB discharge curves measured at different discharge rates (0.25C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C) and different ambient temperatures (−10 °C, 0 °C, 20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C). It should be mentioned that for the 8000 data points used, 70% are used for training, 15% for validation and 15% for testing. After multiple iterations and optimization of the network structure, computational time, processing time and hyperparameters, the ANN model is finalized with 2 hidden layers consisting of 10 neurons in each hidden layer, iteration results are shown in Fig. 5 (a) and tabulated in Table 2 . Levenberg-Marquet algorithm is applied for error propagation using the MATLAB neural network toolbox [18,44,45]. The neurons in the hidden layers use Tansig as the activation function and the last output layer uses the linear activation function. Tansig in MATLAB is just an approximation of Tanh (hyperbolic tangent function) which is defined as follows
(16)
Tansig
( x )
=
2 /
(
1 + exp
(
− 2 x
)
)
− 1

A schematic of the LIB ANN algorithm is represented in Fig. 5(b) and a graphical representation of the tansig activation function and its derivative is shown in Fig. 5(c), associated final ANN hyperparameters are tabulated in Table 3 . The instantaneous current and voltage of the LIB system are measured by the Automated Battery Test Station (ABTS), DOD is estimated from the EKF model, ambient temperature and cell surface temperatures (Surface Temp Ta – anode, Surface Temp Tc – cathode) are measured by the thermocouples. Both the anode and cathode surface temperatures are considered for better accuracy as the anode, cathode temperatures might differ under practical operating conditions. There was no necessity for any pre-processing or post-processing of data as the data acquisition system had minimal interference, low noise and no outliers. Fundamentals of ANN architecture, activation functions, and workings of error back propagation algorithm are listed here [46].

The EKF algorithm is carried out by calibrating the covariance matrix of the measurement noise (R), process noise (Q) and determining the discretization interval. The higher the noise value the lesser the confidence of that aspect, hence depending on the system process noise and sensor measurement noise, initial covariance values should be estimated and tuned. The EKF tuning process is executed by following the calibration guidelines in the literature [47]. The sensor covariance is initially estimated by considering the covariance of the voltage sensor data with the depth of discharge from the experimental data and calibrated further. The covariance for the process noise is estimated relatively to the sensor noise and tuned during the process. Following iterations (calibrations) of these EKF parameters are considered, as shown in Table 4 .

Associated results of the EKF in comparison to direct Coulomb counting (CC) and automated battery test station (ABTS) algorithm are presented in Figs. 6–9
for the DOD changes as a function of discharge time. ABTS Corrected Value refers to the true DOD value measured by the test station, CC refers to the DOD value estimated solely from the Coulomb counting method, EKF-ECM refers to the DOD value estimated by the EKF model using the ECM-based state function, and EKF-Phy-refers to the DOD value estimated by the EKF model using the physics-based state Coulomb counting derived state function.Table 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EKF prediction case 1: measurement noise > process noise
ce:section.ce:para: In the first case, the covariance of the measurement (sensor) noise, is considered to be larger than the covariance of the process noise (state function), here both the physics-based, and ECM-based state function are considered to have the same covariance of the process noise for comparison purposes. The initial state (DOD) is estimated as zero, which in this case is true as the cell is being discharged from full capacity.

As shown in Fig. 6, the DOD estimated by the EKF-Phy-and EKF-ECM is in good correlation with the direct Coulomb Counting (CC) and ATBS measurement with a slight initial undershoot and overshoot. It should also be mentioned that for the present results more confidence is placed on the state function. Both EKF algorithms, despite using different state equations, end up with almost the same DOD estimation after the initial tuning, when the input process noise is the same for both state equations. This indicates a good correlation between the ECM and the physics-based state equation's internal parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EKF prediction case 2: measurement noise < process noise
ce:section.ce:para: In the second case, the covariance of the measurement (sensor) noise is considered to be less than the covariance of the process noise (state function), here both the physics-based and ECM-based state functions are still considered to have the same covariance of the process noise for comparison purposes. The initial state (DOD) is estimated as zero, which in this case is also true.

In Fig. 7, the estimated DOD is still in good correlation with the Coulomb counting method and ABTS measurement, with a slight undershoot especially around the mid and lower end of the prediction. It is also mentioned here that for the present results more confidence is placed on the measurement function. From Figs. 6 and 7, the change in prediction by tuning the covariance value of the process noise and measurement noise can be observed, illustrating the tuning sensitivities of the EKF with a change in associated measurement and process noises.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EKF prediction case 3: faulty initialization of the state
ce:section.ce:para: One of the biggest drawbacks of the direct Coulomb counting method or other bookkeeping methods is that the initialization error and sensor measurement error will accumulate, resulting in increased state error over time. This can be resolved by using these EKF-based state estimation algorithms as shown in Fig. 8 where significantly incorrect state initialization can still converge back to true values over time. In this case, the initial state (DOD) of ECM-based EKF is initiated as 1 and physics-based EKF as 0.5 both of which are incorrect (faulty initialization) as the cell is being discharged from full capacity. This illustrates the advantage of using the EKF estimation method, rather than the direct Coulomb counting method, for example.

Fig. 8 indicates that the initial convergence takes over 1000 s with the improperly tuned initial values of DOD used, and this is too long for automotive or consumer electronics applications. However, this situation can be resolved by further tuning of the parameters (modifying the covariance matrices, discretization time) in the EKF method, as shown in Fig. 9, where the convergence to the true state is within 60 s, even though the initial DOD values are significantly off. Considering the calibration procedure, and test cases it is concluded that the EKF is tuned appropriately and is accurately estimating the final DOD of the LIB despite faulty initialization and using different state functions.

From the above results and discussion, it might be concluded that the EKF performance is sufficiently accurate in estimating the DOD values of the LIB during discharge process. But before introducing the EKF estimation of the DOD, the ANN model performance is evaluated after training by comparing with individual data points (test data) and by also comparing the final heat generation rate predicted by ANN with the experimental value (true value) as shown in Fig. 10 . Here the input to the neural network is exclusively from the experimental data, i.e., instantaneous discharge current, voltage, DOD (estimated by ABTS), ambient temperature and cell surface temperatures (Surface Temp Ta – anode, Surface Temp Tc – cathode). The EKF state prediction (DOD) is not yet introduced to the ANN model to evaluate the independent accuracy of the standalone ANN model (assuming the estimated DOD is perfect).

It is seen from the results presented in Fig. 10 that the standalone ANN model prediction shows excellent performance, when provided with accurate DOD data as input, agreeing well with the measured heat generation rate. Thus it can be concluded that the ANN model is designed and calibrated with sufficient accuracy. Fig. 11 shows the final heat generation rate prediction of the ANN with the DOD estimation by the EKF in comparison with the DOD estimated by the direct Coulomb counting method as well as the true value (experimental results). Notice that the curve CC-ANN refers to the predicted heat generation rate with the DOD calculated directly from the Coulomb counting method and ANN hybrid model, and EKF-ANN refers to the predicted heat generation rate with the DOD estimated from the EKF and ANN hybrid model.

As observed from the results shown in Fig. 11, the predicted instantaneous heat generation rate predicted by the EKF-ANN agrees with the predictions by the Coulomb counting (CC) - ANN, and experimental results (true value). However, minor discrepancies can be observed as the experimental value is calculated at 20 s intervals, whereas the model output is calculated at every second. These discrepancies are nullified if the total heat generation value is considered over the entire period of battery discharge time; as Fig. 11 indicates, a relative error of less than 2% is observed for both the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN model predictions when compared with the experimental value. The relative error between the two models CC-ANN and EKF-ANN predicted heat output value is around 0.39%. This demonstrates that both the EKF-ANN and CC-ANN model predictions are sufficiently accurate, and this hybrid approach of using state estimation with neural network yields accurate results when these two hybrid models are designed and calibrated appropriately with relevant data.

This level of accuracy from the hybrid data-based model can aid in replacing multiple temperature sensors, where such a model can periodically calculate the necessary data required for preliminary control. A hypothetical control situation is considered further to simulate a control system based on the hybrid data-based model predicting total heat generation over a period. Consider a thermal reservoir that can maintain a maximum of 1000 J of heat emitted from the LIB and any further excess heat needs to be dissipated to the environment using a cooling system. A switching case (acting as a thermostat) governed by the output of the hybrid model, can be deployed to control and engage the actuator (radiator fan) facilitating heat dissipation. The total heat output of the LIB over time is obtained by integrating the heat generation rate, cooling system performance and fan status based on the EKF-ANN model are shown in Fig. 12 for the above-mentioned control problem.

From Fig. 12 the total heat in the thermal reservoir with no cooling (no heat dissipation) can be noted to be above 3000 joules and with controlled cooling (heat dissipation by radiator fan) is below 1000 J. The ON/OFF state of the radiator fan representing the actuation of the cooling system can be inferred from the fan status, where the duration of radiator fan engagement and frequency is governed by the total heat output value generated by integrating the output from the hybrid data-based model (EKF-ANN). Here the radiator fan is actuated/engaged (ON) when the reservoir is close to its limit of thermal energy storage, which is determined by the coolant temperature. This control method helps us to forgo the need to measure the temperature of the coolant eliminating a temperature sensor requirement for control purposes. The above results precisely demonstrate the potential of such ANN-based models for real-time thermal management system control and in the development of virtual sensors replacing the need for a physical sensor and reducing the actual system cost and complexity.

Such data-based models can also be used for real-time diagnostics as they are accurate and require relatively less computational power to analyze the deviation or anomaly in a specific situation. A simulated case of the current sensor fault is analyzed as follows: the simulation is carried out by modifying the current signal with an offset of 5 A (creating a bias fault). The fault is affecting the Coulomb counting methodology thereby deviating the estimated DOD, however, the EKF prediction is not affected by the current sensor resulting in no change of the estimated DOD value as shown in Fig. 13 .

The heat generation rate predicted by both the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN models also deviate from the experimental value as the ANN is still affected by the current sensor reading. However, there is a significant change in the relative error and pattern of heat generation rate, and total heat output as shown in Fig. 13, when compared to the experimental value both the CC-ANN and EKF- ANN models have resulted in relative error greater than 50% a significant increase from 2% (when there is no fault). The relative error between the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN predictions has also increased from 0.39% (when there is no fault) to 3.77%. Considering the deviation, the relative error among the models estimating the DOD, heat generation rate, and total heat output a fault can be identified by establishing a threshold limit for the relative error by calibrating across various operating conditions. Similarly, when simulated with a voltage sensor fault (offset of 0.05 V), the DOD estimation by the Coulomb counting method is not affected, however, the EKF which depends on the voltage sensor measurement function loses the track and converges at the end. However, the deviation between the estimated DOD value of the CC-ANN model and the EKF-ANN model still is significant enough and can aid in fault recognition as shown in Fig. 14 .

Similar to the current sensor fault, the predicted heat generation rate from both the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN models also deviate from the experimental value as the ANN will still be affected by the voltage sensor reading. There is also a significant change in the relative error and pattern of heat generation rate and total heat output value as shown in Fig. 14; when compared to the experimental value both the EKF-ANN and CC- ANN models have resulted in a relative error of 48.7% and 27.9%, respectively, a significant increase from 2% (when there is no fault). The relative error between the CC-ANN and EKF-ANN predictions has also increased from 0.39% (when there is no fault) to 16.3%. Considering the deviation, relative error among the models estimating the DOD, heat generation rate, and total heat output a voltage fault can be identified by establishing a threshold limit for the relative error by calibrating across various operating conditions. The following results tabulated in Table 5 demonstrate the potential of such data-based models that can be implemented in real-time for on-board diagnostics by accurately calibrating the relative error between various data-based models and can also further be bolstered by utilizing pattern recognition algorithms for various sensors and operational faults.

In this study, a hybrid data-based model employing artificial neural network (ANN) architecture is developed to predict the heat generation rate and thermal behavior of lithium-ion battery (LIB) with battery discharge current, output voltage, ambient temperature, cell surface temperature, and depth of discharge (DOD) as the feature vectors (inputs), and the DOD is estimated with an extended Kalman filter (EKF), based on the state equations derived from the Coulomb counting (CC) and equivalent circuit model (ECM), respectively, referred to as CC-ANN and ECM-ANN models. The EKFs are tuned accurately for faster convergence of state estimation to the true value irrespective of the state initialization. A shallow neural network utilizing the Marquette-Levenberg algorithm is designed and calibrated using over 8000 cases of the testing data. It is shown that the predicted heat generation rate of LIB by both the CC-ANN and ECM-ANN models agrees well with the experimental results with an accuracy of R2 > 0.995. When compared to the experimental value, the relative error for the total heat output generated is less than 2% for the case of normal sensor operation. Further potential of this hybrid data-based model is evaluated by simulating a thermal management system control and by introducing voltage and current sensor faults for diagnostic purposes. A switching logic emulating a thermostat operated by the output of the developed hybrid data-based model is integrated for the control of the thermal management system, effectively demonstrating the possibility of replacing additional temperature sensors. A simulated case of current sensor fault and voltage sensor fault is implemented and analyzed for the aspect of diagnostics by using CC-ANN and EKF-ANN model output. The relative error of the final heat output value predicted by CC-ANN and EKF-ANN models in comparison to the experimental value is greater than 50% with a current sensor fault and greater than 25% with a voltage sensor fault. This study has demonstrated the potential of such data-based models to monitor, control and diagnose electrochemical systems such as batteries on a real-time basis for field applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work received financial support from Canadian Urban Transit Research and Innovation Consortium (CUTRIC) via Project Number 160028, and Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) via a Discovery Grant.
